title: Cost Structure and the Usefulness of Earnings
abstract: We investigate the relation between the usefulness of earnings and firm cost structure. We document that the usefulness of earnings to external users decreases in the degree of operating leverage, which measures the proportion of fixed to variable costs, and that this decline extends at least partially from the relation between the degree of operating leverage and two earnings properties: aggressive revenue recognition to meet an earnings target and earnings volatility. Our results illustrate how firm fundamentals influence a firm’s information environment and thereby the usefulness of earnings to external users.

author.Brian Cadman: David Eccles School of Business, University of Utah, Salt Lake City, Utah 84112
author.Elia Ferracuti: Fuqua School of Business, Duke University, Durham, North Carolina 27708

article.1. Introduction: A long stream of literature investigates the drivers of variation in the decision usefulness of earnings. Despite a series of studies showing that firm characteristics influence how the accounting system captures economic performance and shapes financial reporting incentives (Schipper and Vincent 2003), research on the relation between firm fundamentals and the usefulness of earnings remains limited (Dechow et al. 2010). We contribute to this literature by studying whether and how cost structure relates to the usefulness of earnings and whether this overall relation is at least partially explained by the association between cost structure and two earnings properties: the use of aggressive revenue recognition to meet earnings targets and earnings volatility. We start by examining the relation between a firm’s cost structure and the usefulness of earnings to external users. To do so, we measure cost structure with the degree of operating leverage, the ratio of fixed to variable costs. The degree of operating leverage increases as the firm invests in fixed costs that reduce the proportion of variable costs, which results in higher con-tribution margins (Horngren et al. 2002). We rely on informativeness and value relevance to capture the usefulness of earnings to external users, where informativeness is the extent to which external users revise their beliefs upon earnings announcements, and value relevance is the extent to which earnings reflect the information revealed during the fiscal year (Collins and Kothari 1989).1 Ex ante, the relation between cost structure and earnings usefulness is unclear. On the one hand, investors may respond more strongly to earnings surprises by firms with higher degrees of operating leverage because high fixed costs create barriers to entry, which increases the correlation between earnings surprises and future cash flows (Ahmed 1994). Investors may also respond more strongly to earnings surprises by firms with higher degrees of operating leverage because a dollar surprise in revenues contributes a larger amount to income (or covering fixed expenses), which increases investors’ revisions of future cash flows (Ertimur et al. 2003). On the other hand, earnings may be less useful with higher degrees of operating leverage for at least two reasons. First, operating leverage is positively associated with systematic risk (Lev 1974), which increases the discount rate that investors apply to firms’ future cash flows and reduces the market response to earnings surprises (Biddle and Seow 1991,Vuolteenaho 2002). Second, literature shows that earnings usefulness depends on earnings properties (e.g.,Collins and Kothari 1989). As firms invest in fixed costs that reduce variable costs, earnings become more sensitive to changes in demand, which (i) enhances incentives to aggressively recognize revenue to meet an earnings target and (ii) increases earnings volatility. Because these two properties make earnings a noisier signal of performance, the usefulness of earnings may decline in the degree of operating leverage. Consistent with the informativeness of earnings declining in the degree of operating leverage, we find that investors and analysts revise their expectations less in response to the earnings surprises of firms with higher degrees of operating leverage. We also find that higher operating leverage is associated with a lower correlation between annual earnings and annual returns, consistent with earnings being less value-relevant. These results indicate that the net effect of operating leverage on earnings usefulness is negative. We next assess whether earnings properties contribute to this relation. We first establish the association between cost structure and two earnings properties indicated by the literature as potentially important drivers of earnings usefulness: the incentive to aggressively recognize revenue to meet a performance target and earnings volatility. Firms with a higher degree of operating leverage exhibit greater contribution margins and need fewer incremental sales to meet an earnings target that they would otherwise miss. As a consequence, we expect firms with higher degrees of operating leverage to more aggressively recognize revenue to meet an earnings target. Consistent with this prediction, we document a more positive association between aggressive revenue recognition and the degree of operating leverage for firms that just meet or beat earnings targets relative to firms that barely miss these targets. We examine other instances that create an incentive for managers to manipulate earnings upward: stock-for-stock acquisitions, seasoned equity offerings, and the inclusion of earnings-related covenants in loan contracts. We find a positive association between aggressive revenue recognition and the degree of operating leverage in these instances as well. We also expect that firms with higher degrees of operating leverage display more volatile earnings because fixed costs increase the contribution of revenue to earnings. Consistent with this conjecture, with prior empirical evidence (e.g.,Lev 1983andAboody et al. 2017), and with common beliefs (e.g.,Horngren et al. 2002), we document that earnings volatility increases in the degree of operating leverage. Although this test is not novel to the literature, we include it to confirm these earlier findings using our proxies and sample. We then study whether these relations between cost structure and earnings properties drive the association between cost structure and the usefulness of earnings. We combine the theoretical determinants of earnings response coefficients inMiller and Rock (1985)with a recursive structural equation model (path analysis; e.g.,Bhattacharya et al. 2011) and obtain three main insights. First, the usefulness of earnings to shareholders declines in the degree of operating leverage through both earnings properties and other channels. Second, the earnings properties channel appears economically important, as it explains approximately 50% of variation in the usefulness of earnings due to cost structure. Third, both the incentive to aggressively recognize revenue to meet an earnings target and earnings volatility play a role; earnings volatility explains 95% of the effect, while the incentive for aggressive revenue recognition accounts for the remaining 5%. The last result suggests that, on average, the market imposes a small penalty on high-operating-leverage firms for their incentive to aggressively recognize revenue around performance targets. However, the small penalty may arise because firms rarely face incentives to aggressively recognize revenue. For example, firms meet or barely beat zero earnings in only 4.5% of the observations in our sample. Thus, we investigate whether the penalty applied by markets to firms with incentives to aggressively recognize revenue is sizeable when the incentive materializes. We find that high-operating-leverage firms that report earnings per share within $0.01 of an earnings target experience cumulative abnormal returns up to half a percentage point lower than low-operating-leverage firms that report similar earnings surprises. This result suggests that the penalty applied by investors to firms with an incentive to engage in aggressive revenue recognition is economically meaningful. Our conceptual framework predicts that cost structure causes variation in the usefulness of earnings. Nevertheless, our analyses reflect associations between operating leverage and earnings usefulness and do not allow for causal inferences because of the endogenous nature of cost structure decisions. For example, managers may consider the effects of cost structure on earnings properties when making cost structure decisions, thus creating reverse causality concerns. To mitigate these concerns, we rely on the grant of permanent normal trade relations (PNTR) to China, which eliminated uncertainty about tariff rates on Chinese imports, as a source of plausibly exogenous variation in the degree of operating leverage (Chen et al. 2019). We find that firms with greater exposure to tariffs with China experience a relative increase in operating leverage and that these same firms also report earnings that are less useful, more aggressively recognize revenue to meet an earnings target, and experience higher earnings volatility following the granting of PNTR to China. We conclude the study by exploring a potential implication of our findings. We conjecture and show that the boards of directors of firms with higher degrees of operating leverage rely relatively less on earnings when making turnover decisions. This evidence indicates that the relation between cost structure and earnings properties has the potential to influence the way earnings are used not only for investment purposes, but also for contracting purposes. Overall, we contribute to the literature by studying whether firm cost structure, an important firm characteristic, shapes earnings usefulness through its relation with earnings properties. Our results support the notion that cost structure affects the usefulness of earnings and that this effect stems at least partially from the relation between cost structure and earnings properties. Prior studies have offered multiple—sometimes conflicting—conjectures for the relation between cost structure and earnings usefulness, but have not investigated these channels (Biddle and Seow 1991,Ahmed 1994,Ertimur et al. 2003). As such, our paper offers two insights to this literature. First, we identify an unexplored channel through which cost structure relates with earnings usefulness, the relation with earnings properties. Second, we document the economic importance of earnings properties in explaining variation in the relation between cost structure and earnings usefulness relative to alternative channels. These insights speak to two broad streams of literature. We contribute to the literature on the economic determinants of earnings usefulness by documenting that cost structure is one such determinant, which broadens our understanding of the channels that link firm fundamentals to the usefulness of earnings. Furthermore, by studying not only the relation between firm fundamentals and earnings usefulness, but also an unexplored potential channel for this relation, earnings properties, we are able to provide deeper insights that would otherwise be difficult to gauge (Dechow et al. 2010). Thus, we contribute to the broad literature on the determinants and consequences of earnings properties (Collins and Kothari 1989,Dhaliwal et al. 1991,Hayn 1995,Core and Schrand 1999). Second, we contribute to a growing literature that links financial and management accounting issues. Starting withHemmer and Labro (2008), researchers have been interested in gaining a better understanding of the relation between management accounting decisions and financial accounting outcomes. Research shows that superior internal information systems lead to more accurate management forecasts (Cassar and Gibson 2008,Dorantes et al. 2013), better tax planning (Gallemore and Labro 2015) and investment (Binz et al. 2023), and that common errors in accruals are caused by poor internal information available to managers (Gong et al. 2009). We contribute to this literature by showing that cost structure, a real management accounting choice, also produces important financial accounting consequences.
article.2. Theoretical Framework and Empirical P: Understanding the interplay between firm fundamentals, earnings properties, and the decision usefulness of earnings is at the core of accounting research (Schipper and Vincent 2003). The literature offers mixed predictions and evidence on the relation between the cost structure and the usefulness of earnings. For this reason, we first investigate the relation between the degree of operating leverage and the usefulness of earnings in our sample. We then consider whether the relation between cost structure and earnings properties helps explain variation in earnings usefulness, a channel that remains unexplored in the literature. We focus on the degree of operating leverage, where the degree of operating leverage increases as the firm invests in fixed costs that reduce the proportion of variable costs, which results in higher contribution margins (Horngren et al. 2002). 2.1. Cost Structure and the Usefulness of Earnings The literature offers multiple predictions for the relation between cost structure and the usefulness of earnings. Fixed costs create barriers to entry, increasing earnings persistence and, thereby, the correlation between earnings surprises and future cash flows. In addition, investments in fixed costs that reduce variable costs increase contribution margins, such that surprises in revenues have a greater impact on income. As such, investors revise their expectations about future cash flows to a greater extent as the degree of operating leverage increases. Consistent with this prediction,Ahmed (1994)shows that the earnings response coefficient increases in the ratio of selling, general, and administrative (SG&A) expenses over total operating costs, andErtimur et al. (2003)document that investors’ response to revenue surprises increases in operating leverage. Therefore, these studies suggest that a greater degree of operating leverage is associated with more useful earnings. Another stream of literature suggests that the usefulness of earningsdeclinesin the degree of operating leverage. First, this relation may emerge because of the association between cost structure and risk.Lev (1974)documents both analytically and empirically that a higher ratio of fixed to variable costs increases systematic risk—namely, the covariance between firm and market returns. Because investors use earnings to update their priors not only about expected future cash flows, but also about the riskiness of those future cash flows (Beaver et al. 1970,Vuolteenaho 2002), they will apply a higher discount rate to the future cash flows of firms with higher operating leverage and, accordingly, reduce their response to earnings surprises. Consistent with this conjecture,Biddle and Seow (1991)find that earnings response coefficients decline in operating leverage. Second, the negative relation between degree of operating leverage and earnings usefulness may emerge through earnings properties, as we illustrate inSection 2.2. 2.2. Cost Structure and Earnings Properties Although the relation between cost structure and earnings usefulness is unclear, we can make more precise predictions about how cost structure influences the usefulness of earnings through earnings properties. In this way, we also answer the call inDechow et al. (2010)to rely on a complete path approach, rather than examining either determinants or consequences of earnings properties in isolation. This complete path approach allows for deeper insights into the relation between firm fundamentals and the usefulness of earnings. Literature documents that earnings properties can explain variation in the usefulness of earnings. For example,Kormendi and Lipe (1987)demonstrate both analytically and empirically that investors respond more strongly to more persistent earnings, a result confirmed byCollins and Kothari (1989). We expect cost structure to relate to two properties that reduce the quality of earnings as a signal of performance and, thus, their usefulness to outsiders: managers’ aggressive revenue recognition to meet an earnings target and earnings volatility. We propose that the relation between cost structure and earnings usefulness arises because cost structure influences aggressive revenue recognition. As the firm’s cost structure shifts from variable costs to fixed costs—for example, through investments in a machine that reduces labor hours—each incremental sale has a larger effect on net income. Thus, holding the underlying economic performance constant, firms with higher operating leverage need fewer incremental sales to meet an earnings target.2As a consequence, firms with larger fixed costs face lower costs of meeting an earnings target, as long as the cost of this aggressive revenue recognition behavior increases in the sales required to meet an earnings target. There are at least two reasons why this is the case. First, auditors set materiality thresholds before performing their auditing activities (International Standards on Auditing 320, paragraph 10), so smaller amounts of sales manipulation are less likely to meet the materiality threshold and result in further investigation by the auditor. Second, as long as sales manipulation involves suboptimal business choices, large amounts of sales manipulation will result in worse future performance and hurt firm value in the long term. Because managers have a preference for lower-cost earnings management opportunities (Cohen et al. 2008,Zang 2012), we predict that the probability of aggressive revenue recognition to meet an earnings target increases with the degree of operating leverage and that this association reduces the usefulness of earnings.3 Stein (1989)predicts that investors discount the earnings of firms that meet an earnings target when these firms face strong incentives to meet the target. Empirical evidence supports this theory by showing that managers respond to incentives from performance thresholds by managing earnings (Bizjak et al. 2015) and that market participants anticipate this response and display more skepticism toward earnings surprises because manipulated earnings will not persist (Coles et al. 2006). To the extent that the use of aggressive revenue recognition to meet a performance target increases with the degree of operating leverage and that the market anticipates this behavior, investors will discount earnings reported by firms that just meet earnings targets more as the degree of operating leverage increases. We also propose that the relation between cost structure and earnings usefulness arises through earnings volatility. Operating leverage increases the sensitivity of earnings to changes in demand because investments in fixed cost that reduce variable costs increase contribution margins. Furthermore, higher fixed costs, which do not vary as a function of economic activity, may also lower the quality of matching between revenues and costs, which increases earnings volatility (Dichev and Tang 2008). Consistent with this characterization, prior literature documents that earnings volatility increases in the degree of operating leverage (Lev 1983,Aboody et al. 2017). As a consequence, we predict that higher degrees of operating leverage are associated with more volatile earnings streams. Literature suggests that more volatile earnings streams are less informative to external users.Dichev and Tang (2009)document that more volatile earnings are less predictable, which makes them a noisier signal of performance (Dechow and Dichev 2002). Noisier performance signals increase the information uncertainty faced by investors—uncertainty that cannot be diversified away when interpreting earnings news (Easley and O’Hara 2004). Therefore, to the extent that the degree of operating leverage is associated with more volatile earnings and more information uncertainty, the earnings of firms with higher operating leverage will be a less useful signal of economic performance to external users. In summary, we expect that the usefulness of earnings declines in the degree of operating leverage through positive relations between the operating leverage and both aggressive revenue recognition and earnings volatility.Figure 1graphically illustrates these predictions.
article.3. Data and Measurement: 3.1. Sample Description We study the relation between cost structure and earnings usefulness using a sample of industrial firms with nonmissing data for the variables included in our analyses. We start with 52,767 firm-year observations (9,495 firms) over the period 1988–2019, for which we can estimate cost structure using the approach described inSection 3.2.4The sample drops to 39,640 firm-year observations (6,966 firms) once we require nonmissing data necessary to calculate variables from Compustat and CRSP. Finally, the sample shrinks to 27,486 firm-year observations (5,170 firms) after we require nonmissing data on analyst forecasts from I/B/E/S that are necessary to calculate earnings surprises. We report descriptive statistics inTable 1. The average firm in our sample has a logarithm of lagged total assets of 6.44, leverage of 0.203, and intangible assets representing 17% of assets. Of these firms, 40% distribute dividends, while the average stock beta is 1.3. These amounts are consistent with descriptive statistics reported in similar studies, such asChen et al. (2019). Measurement of Cost Structure Cost structure is unobservable because generally accepted accounting principles do not require firms to disclose fixed and variable costs in financial statements or to disclose units sold. We overcome this difficulty with two proxies based on estimation approaches used in the literature: the levels approach, as inLev (1974), and the elasticities approach, as inNoreen and Soderstrom (1994), both of which assume that operating costs increase linearly in firm activity.5We implement these approaches measuring total operating costs as the sum of cost of goods sold, depreciation and amortization, and selling, general, and administrative expenses, and firm activity using operating revenue.6 The levels approach relies on a rolling time-series regression of the level of total operating costs on the level of operating revenues, estimated over 12 quarters. The intercept resulting from this regression represents our estimate of fixed costs, which we then divide by total operating costs to arrive at the degree of operating leverage (DOL_Lev).7The second proxy relies on a rolling time-series regression of the natural logarithm of total operating costs on the natural logarithm of operating revenues. As discussed inNoreen and Soderstrom (1994), the coefficient associated with the natural logarithm of operating revenue (𝛽β) represents the ratio of marginal to average cost, which assuming linearity is equivalent to the ratio of variable to total costs. We follow the literature (Noreen and Soderstrom 1994,Kallapur and Eldenburg 2005,Aboody et al. 2017) and use1−𝛽1−βas our second measure of the degree of operating leverage (DOL_NS). The elasticities approach has two advantages over the first proxy. It is theoretically consistent with a generalized Cobb-Douglas production function, and it does not suffer from heteroscedastic residuals, which bias standard errors when assessing the statistical significance of the𝛽βcoefficient. However, estimates from the log-log approach are difficult to convert to monetary values of fixed and variable costs, which is one advantage of the level-level approach. We also assess the sensitivity of our results to using the high-low approach (Horngren et al. 2002). We postpone a discussion of this measure toSection 5.3. article.4. Empirical Design and Results: 4.1. Cost Structure and the Usefulness of Earnings In our first empirical analysis, we study the relation between cost structure and the usefulness of earnings to two users of accounting information: shareholders and financial analysts. As discussed inSection 2, cost structure can influence the usefulness of earnings through multiple channels. Because these channels have differing implications, we do not make a directional prediction for the relation between cost structure and earnings usefulness. We test this relation using the following empirical model: We define earnings usefulness in two ways that extend from the Statement of Financial Accounting Concepts No. 2: earnings informativeness (i.e., predictive value) and value relevance (i.e., confirmatory value). We define earnings informativeness as the extent to which capital market participants revise their beliefs when unexpected earnings are released (Collins and Kothari 1989) and measure it with two dependent variables. Our first dependent variable is the abnormal stock returns accumulated over the three days around the earnings announcement day (CAR), which identifies the extent to which shareholders revise their expectations in response to earnings news. Our second dependent variable is the natural logarithm of 1 plus the change in earnings per share (EPS) forecast for the subsequent year issued by analysts during the seven days following current year’s earnings announcement (𝐴𝑛𝑎𝑙𝑦𝑠𝑡𝑠𝑅𝑒𝑣AnalystsRev), which identifies the extent to which analysts revise their expectations in response to earnings news.8We define value relevance as the extent to which earnings reflect events and information about the firm accrued during the fiscal year (Collins and Kothari 1989). We measure it using abnormal stock returns over the fiscal year (BHAR), calculated as buy-and-hold abnormal returns compounded over the 12-month period ending 3 months after the fiscal year-end. To calculate abnormal returns, we use an expected return model based on size, book-to-market, and momentum, adjusted for delisting (Beaver et al. 2007).UErepresents earnings news, which we measure with the difference between reported earnings per share and the most recent I/B/E/S consensus forecast available before the earnings announcement, scaled by price. Finally,DOLis eitherDOL_LevorDOL_NS, calculated as described inSection 3. We expect the estimated coefficient associated with earnings news to be positive (𝛽1>0β1>0), as established in the earnings response coefficient literature. The coefficient of interest to us is the interaction betweenUEandDOL(𝛽3β3), which describes how the usefulness of earnings varies with the degree of operating leverage. A positive coefficient would indicate that earnings usefulness increases in operating leverage, whereas a negative coefficient would indicate the opposite. We complement the model by including a vector of firm characteristics that literature shows is associated with the stock market reaction to earnings (e.g.,Core and Schrand 1999,Chen et al. 2014,Wang 2014): the absolute value of accruals (|Accruals|) and the absolute value of special items (|Sp Items|) to control for differences in the persistence of earnings; stock beta (Beta), the market-to-book ratio (Mtb), stock return volatility (Returns Vol), the yearly percentage change in revenue (Sales Ch), and the volatility of quarterly revenue calculated over the last 12 quarters (Sales Vol) to control for differences in firm riskiness; an indicator variable equal to 1 if the firm distributes dividends during the year, and 0 otherwise (Dividends), the ratio of intangible assets over total assets (Intang), leverage (Leverage), the life-cycle stage of the firm calculated as inCollins et al. (2014)(Life Cycle), and the logarithm of total assets (Size) to control in differences in business model among firms. We also interact each control variable withUEto address the possibility that these variables introduce nonlinearities in how capital market participants respond to earnings news. Finally, we include industry fixed effects to control for any time-invariant industry-specific factor influencing the usefulness of earnings and year fixed effects to control for variation in the uncertainty of the economic environment over time. Together, these results indicate that the usefulness of earnings to external users declines in the degree of operating leverage. 4.2. Cost Structure and the Usefulness of Earnings: The Earnings Properties Channel We next explore whether the association between cost structure and the usefulness of earnings documented in the previous section emerges, at least in part, from the association between cost structure and two properties of a firm’s earnings: aggressive revenue recognition to meet performance targets and volatility. 4.2.1. Cost Structure and Aggressive Revenue Recognition to Meet a Performance Target. We predict that because firms with higher degrees of operating leverage need fewer costly incremental sales to meet an earnings target that they would otherwise miss, these firms use aggressive revenue recognition to meet their targets. We test this prediction with the following empirical model: We followStubben (2010)and measure aggressive revenue recognition with𝐷𝑖𝑠𝑐𝑟𝑅𝑒𝑣DiscrRev, the residual from a regression of account receivables on revenues.10Following the literature (Burgstahler and Dichev 1997,Degeorge et al. 1999), we identify the zero-earnings threshold, last year’s EPS, and analysts’ consensus forecast as important earnings targets for managers.11Accordingly, we defineSuspectas an indicator variable equal to 1 when earnings per share meet or beat the earnings threshold by $0.01, and 0 if earnings per share miss the earnings threshold by $0.01. This approach reduces the risk that firms just meeting a performance threshold differ from firms in the general population in a way that influences our test results (Dechow et al. 2003).DOLis one of our two proxies for the degree of operating leverage (DOL_LevandDOL_NS), calculated as before. We follow related research (e.g.,Roychowdhury 2006,Doyle et al. 2013) and control for the following variables: the absolute value of accruals (|Accruals|); an indicator variable equal to 1 if the firm distributes dividends during the year, and 0 otherwise (Dividends); the ratio of intangible assets over total assets (Intang); leverage (Leverage), calculated as short- and long-term debt over total assets; the life cycle stage of the firm (Life Cycle); the market-to-book ratio (Mtb); the yearly percentage change in revenue (Sales Ch); the volatility of quarterly revenue calculated over the last 12 quarters (Sales Vol); the logarithm of total assets (Size); and the absolute value of special items (|Sp Items|). Each control variable is interacted withSuspectto account for nonlinearities in the relation between aggressive revenue recognition and each control variable. We further include industry and year fixed effects to control for remaining industry time-invariant or time-varying unobservable differences. Table 6presents results from estimatingEquation (2). We expect𝛽3β3to be positive if aggressive revenue recognition to meet an earnings target increases with the degree of operating leverage. Finally, the sign and significance of coefficients on the control variables, which we do not report for conciseness, align with those found in related studies (e.g.,Doyle et al. 2013). Together, the results show that aggressive revenue recognition increases with the degree of operating leverageonlywhen firms have an incentive to meet an earnings target. Permanent Normal Trade Relations to China Our tests implicitly assume that cost structurecausesvariation in the usefulness of earnings. Path analysis models the structural components of earnings usefulness, thus increasing the likelihood that our evidence is causal. However, this solution may be inadequate if managers consider the effects of cost structure on earnings properties when making cost structure decisions, which our path analysis does not explicitly model. To address this concern, we follow prior research and use the granting of permanent normal trade relations to China as a source of plausibly exogenous variation in cost structure (Chen et al. 2019). Under the Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act of 1930, U.S. import tariffs on goods from nonmarket economies such as China were subject to tariff rates that were much higher than the Normal Trade Relations (NTR) tariffs applied to World Trade Organization (WTO) members. However, the U.S. Trade Act of 1974 gave the President of the United States the possibility to grant NTR tariff rates to nonmarket economies for one year and Congress the possibility to renew these rates every year. In the 1980s, U.S. presidents started granting NTR tariff rates to China, considerably reducing tariff rates applied to imported Chinese goods. This granting, however, was highly contentious, and its yearly renewal was uncertain. For example, during the 10-year period that spans 1990–1999, the U.S. House of Representatives voted to interrupt this practice on three occasions, but the special status was not overturned because the U.S. Senate failed to sustain the House vote. Thus, there was significant uncertainty around the yearly renewal of NTR tariff rates to China. This resulted in considerable uncertainty over the input costs faced by U.S. firms that sourced goods from China, which ultimately reduced these firms’ incentives to invest in capital-intensive (rather than labor-intensive) production technologies (Pierce and Schott 2016). In response to China joining the WTO in 1999, the U.S. Congress passed a bill granting Permanent Normal Trade Relations status to China in 2000, thereby eliminating uncertainty surrounding the renewal of the tariff waiver, while leaving tariff rates applied to Chinese goods unchanged. This change in U.S. foreign policy has two features that make it well suited as a plausibly exogenous shock to the degree of operating leverage. First, the bill eliminated uncertainty over the input costs faced by U.S. firms that sourced goods from China. As uncertainty over input costs depresses irreversible investments (Dixit and Pindyck 1994), the removal of this uncertainty induced U.S. firms to invest in capital- or skill-intensive production technologies, thereby increasing their operating leverage (Chen et al. 2019). Second, the difference between the non-NTR rates to which tariffs would have risen if annual renewal had failed (which average 37% in 1999) and the NTR tariff rates that were locked in by PNTR (which average 4% in 1999), also known as the Normal Trade Relations Gap (NTRGap), varied widely across industries. Because firms in industries with larger Normal Trade Relations Gap faced larger consequences in their input costs from changes in the tariffs applied to China, these firms stood to benefit more from the resolution of uncertainty induced by the granting of PNTR status to China. We exploit these features in a generalized difference-in-differences research design that studies how the difference in outcome variable (cost structure, earnings properties, earnings usefulness) associated with the industry-level Normal Trade Relations Gap (first difference) varies with the granting of PNTR status to China (second difference): 𝑁𝑇𝑅𝐺𝑎𝑝𝑖𝑛𝑑NTRGapindis the difference between non-NTR industry tariff rates to which tariffs would have risen if annual renewal had failed and the NTR industry tariff rates that were locked in by PNTR.𝑃𝑜𝑠𝑡𝑡Posttis an indicator variable set to 1 during the 10 years following the passage of the bill granting PNTR status to China (i.e., 2000), and 0 during the 10 years prior. The model includes the same set of controls described above inEquation (2)and both industry and year fixed effects, which absorb the𝑁𝑇𝑅𝐺𝑎𝑝𝑖𝑛𝑑NTRGapindand𝑃𝑜𝑠𝑡𝑡Posttstandalone coefficients. We first confirm the results inChen et al. (2019)andPierce and Schott (2016)that firms in industries with largerNTRGapincrease their operating leverage more than firms in industries with lowerNTRGapfollowing the granting of PNTR status to China.Table 10, columns (1) and (2) document a positive difference-in-differences estimator, which is consistent with the notion that the resolution of uncertainty about tariff rates associated with the granting of PNTR status to China induced firms with more exposure to this uncertainty to invest in capital- or skill-intensive production technologies. We allow the relation betweenNTRGapand the degree of operating leverage to vary every two-year period and report results inFigure 5. We note two important insights from the figure. First, firms in industries with a largerNTRGapshow a similar pre-PNTR trend in operating leverage relative to firms in industries with lowerNTRGap, which supports the identifying assumption of parallel trends in the outcome variable implicit in a difference-in-differences identification strategy. Second, the difference-in-differences estimator turns positive starting with the years 2001 and 2002, consistent with firms adjusting their cost structure fairly quickly in response to the granting of PNTR status to China. article.5. Other Analyses: 5.1. Implications: Cost Structure and CEO Turnover Decisions We study a potential implication of our main evidence: the possibility that cost structure influences the way boards of directors use earnings information in turnover decisions. Prior research shows that earnings performance explains CEO turnover (Weisbach 1988,Murphy and Zimmerman 1993) and that this association depends on earnings properties. For example,Engel et al. (2003)document that accounting information receives greater weight in turnover decision when accounting numbers are more useful, as measured by their precision and sensitivity. Starting from this result, we predict that the board of directors’ reliance on earnings performance in turnover decisions declines in a firm’s degree of operating leverage because operating leverage lowers the usefulness of those earnings as signals of management performance. To test this prediction, we followEngel et al. (2003)and regress an indicator for forced CEO turnover, measured using data fromPeters and Wagner (2014), on industry-adjusted earnings and industry-adjusted stock returns as signals of CEO performance. Consistent with prior literature,Table 11shows that the likelihood that a CEO is fired declines in industry-adjusted accounting (Adj Roa) and stock market (Adj Stock Ret) performance. We also observe that the board’s reliance on earnings declines in the degree of operating leverage, consistent with earnings being a less useful signal of management performance for those firms: the interaction betweenAdj RoaandDOLis always positive, as expected, and statistically different from 0 at conventional significance levels. Interestingly, at the same time, the board of directors seems to place more weight on stock returns as a signal of CEO performance: the interaction betweenAdj Stock RetandDOLis always negative and significant. These results indicate a shift of weight away from accounting performance metrics and toward market-based performance metrics as operating leverage increases. Importance of Aggressive Revenue Recognition Incentives Our path analysis indicates that the majority of the effect of cost structure on earnings usefulness via earnings properties arises from earnings volatility. This evidence may cast doubts on the economic significance of the incentive for aggressive revenue recognition to meet an earnings target as a determinant of earnings usefulness. We note, however, that this evidence is consistent with the nature of the incentive: firms have an incentive to engage in aggressive revenue recognition only when their economic performance would otherwise fall short of that target, an event that occurs infrequently. For example, observations that meet or barely beat zero earnings are approximately 4.5%; those that meet or barely beat last year’s EPS are less than 1.5%, whereas meeting or just beating analyst forecasts occurs in approximately 10% of our sample. Therefore, on average, the market imposes a small penalty on high-operating-leverage firms for their incentive to aggressively recognize revenue around performance targets because this incentive materializes with low probability. To consider this, we investigate how the market reaction to earnings announcements (CAR) changes as a function of operating leverage for different levels of earnings surprises. We conjecture that shareholder skepticism about the underlying economic performance of a firm is larger for firms with a high degree of operating leverage that just meet or just miss the analyst earnings target because these firms had stronger incentives to aggressively recognize revenue to meet the target (Shin 1994). We test this conjecture by regressingCARonDOL, one of our metrics of operating leverage, on the subsamples of firms reporting a difference between actual and forecasted EPS of [−$0.05 to −$0.02], [−$0.01 to $0.01], and [$0.02 to $0.05]. We expect the market reaction to earnings announcements to decline in the degree of operating leverage more strongly just around the earnings target (i.e., earnings surprise between −$0.01 and $0.01). Table 12reports coefficient estimates forDOL_Levin columns (1)–(3) andDOL_NSin columns (4)–(6). The estimated relation betweenCARandDOLis negative and statistically significant only for firms around the earnings target. The effect is economically meaningful: a one-standard-deviation increase in operating leverage is associated with a decline in cumulative abnormal returns of nearly half a percentage point. These results support the notion that market participants recognize that the incentive and opportunity to meet an earnings threshold using aggressive revenue recognition increases with the degree of operating leverage and penalize these firms considerably more when they barely meet or miss an earnings target. Sensitivity Analyses We perform additional tests to assess the sensitivity of our results to alternative empirical choices. In all of these tests, we re-estimateEquations (1), (2), and (3) and report estimated coefficients in columns (1)–(3), (4), and (5), respectively. First, we recognize that our path analysis and the difference-in-differences design are imperfect ways to address endogeneity concerns. For this reason, we perform two additional tests to further mitigate these concerns. InAppendix B, we tighten our fixed-effect structure by replacing industry and year with industry-by-year fixed effects. In this way, we are better able to control for trends in cost structure driven by industry-level shocks, such as shifts in consumer taste or technological innovation, but face the risk of soaking out helpful variation to identify the relation between cost structure and our variables of interest. We also consider the possibility that observable firm characteristics are correlated with both cost structure and our outcome variables. Thus, inAppendix C, we use entropy balancing (Hainmueller 2012). Our inferences are robust to both approaches. These results notwithstanding, we recognize that these approaches are imperfect solutions to endogeneity problems, and, therefore, our results should be interpreted with caution. Second, we acknowledge that measures of operating leverage are inherently prone to measurement error and that both of our proxies rely on a time-series estimation approach, which may reflect changes in cost structure slowly. InAppendix D, we introduce a proxy based on the high-low approach, which estimates operating leverage using only the two quarters with the highest and lowest revenues during the year (Horngren et al. 2002). Our findings continue to hold, although they are somewhat statistically weaker, when using this proxy, which reflects changes in cost structure more quickly at the cost of more measurement error.
article.6. Conclusion: We study whether and how cost structure, an important firm characteristic, influences the usefulness of earnings to outsiders and the extent to which this influence emerges from the relation between cost structure and earnings properties. We measure cost structure with the degree of operating leverage, the ratio of fixed to variable costs, and document three main findings. First, cost structure is associated with lower earnings usefulness. Second, this association is mediated by the relation between cost structure and two earnings properties: firms’ incentive to use aggressive revenue recognition to meet a performance target and earnings volatility. Third, these relations are possibly causal. We also document an important implication of our findings related to the use of accounting performance and CEO turnover that opens the door to possible extensions of our work. Through these findings, the paper improves our understanding of the relation between firm fundamentals and the usefulness of earnings and indicates that earnings properties are an important channel driving that relation. Furthermore, we contribute to the growing literature that links financial and management accounting by showing that cost structure has important financial accounting and information consequences.
